text: 7/10 - Painting Potential Solid "friendslop" fun with more potential! Gameplay Pose in different shapes and paint yourself to blend in your environment and hope you don't get found by the hunters! Very simple but great spin on a prop hunt/hide and seek game! Visuals Simple effective visual style - perfect for a hiding game. Only issue have is some lights are as bright as the sun and make it very hard to see anything in that area - very cheeky when people hide there knowing that! Sounds Hoping for more taunt sounds in the future. Linux Support Worked perfectly fine on CachyOS with native proton package. Improvements The core gameplay loop of this game is great, however there's some issues that need improvements. UI - It's very clunky, could definitely do with a freshen up to make things smoother. Steam invite support - Why can't I invite my friends if hosting private server? Server hosting/browsing - Lot of my friends struggled to join me - Needing full case sensitive name and password inputted to find server seems bit awkward. Have good faith in dev to continue working hard on this game, as only after 1 day we have workshop custom map support and a newly added score table tracking who has been seen the most without being found!

I haven't played much yet, but I do need to point out the negative reviews. Firstly, this game is amazing. It's an original standalone prop hunt game that has a unique twist you won't find anywhere. It's on the level of Among Us and other similar games where it is better with friends, but you do have the option for public games which can still be fun. I have noticed some negative reviews mentioning brush stroke size and characters always looking shiny when painting and trying to hide. I just want to mention, PLEASE for the love of god actually read the ui keybinds that are right in front of your face. You can change brush stroke size by right-clicking and dragging. They literally tell you that and some people still can't figure it out. How? And as for the shininess of the paint, just adjust the roughness. Roughness = Matte and Metallic = Shiny. Have none of you tried the different options? I have 4 hours in this game and figured it out after the first game I tried. Please for the love of god try some things before leaving an inaccurate negative review to a great game. And lastly, recent reviews have mentioned it is awful trying to play with friends because you can't directly invite them, or the search function doesn't work. I personally haven't had issues yet, but the dev also recently update to allow inviting to your private games. All around, this game does have bugs but is still worth the money. If the price tag was $20 I would still buy it and get it for friends. This game is amazing and every update from now on will only be an improvement. I highly recommend this game especially if you have friends!
Honestly, anyone complaining about this game just doesn't know how to have fun. This game is a blast.

The game is honestly really fun and highly enjoyable, but it currently suffers from several major issues and bugs that completely ruin the experience. Here is a breakdown of the problems that need to be fixed as soon as possible: ​Griefing from the Lobby: Players who are still in the lobby can open their mics or type in chat to reveal the hiders' exact locations to the hunter. This completely ruins the fun of active matches. ​Spectator Issues: When new players join the lobby while a match is already running, they can see the entire map and everyone's locations. ​No Ban System & Game-Breaking Exploits: There are many toxic players using foul language, as well as hackers who ruin the gameplay. Even if we kick them, they can easily rejoin the server instantly because there is no proper ban system. Furthermore, some players somehow manage to join mid-match and remain completely invisible as hiders until the very end. They are impossible to find, and we can't even check the player list to see if they are actually in the game or not. ​Broken Settings & Controls: The settings menu doesn't work properly. For instance, you cannot customize keybinds or even change your microphone input device. ​No Way to Play with Friends: Playing with friends is a nightmare. There is no server search bar, no direct invite system, and no reliable way to ensure you can all join the same server together. ​Forced Background Music: The game music is mandatory, and there is no volume slider to lower it or turn it off. ​Deafening Lobby Sounds: The whistling feature in the lobby becomes incredibly annoying and loud when everyone starts spamming it at the same time, and we have no option to mute or adjust its volume. ​Conclusion: The core game loop has amazing potential and is very entertaining, but the developers desperately need to fix these quality-of-life, exploit, and moderation features. I hope to see these updates soon!

This game is super fun with friends, but there's a few things that seriously turned me off from giving a better review. The multiplayer system is actually ass. You cannot invite friends normally and have to input a server name and password and refresh for awhile til it pops up. I'm also very disappointed by the fact there is no filter for server names, passwords, and probably the chat. When you look at the public servers, all the names are super discriminatory. Most of the public servers are named something with a slur, super racist, sexist, homophobic, or saying they like to touch children. It's also very glitchy and the "you're too hidden" mechanic is not very good. It triggers even when you're in a painfully visible spot. The UI could be much better as well. Higher graphic settings are an eyesore, especially when you're hunting or trying to match your color to your hiding spot. There's so many issues with reflections and my group and I found the game is much more bearable to play when you lower your graphics. At minimum I was expecting a proper multiplayer system instead of whatever the hell it currently is. This game has a lot of potential, but you get what you pay for. I will likely play some more of this since its pretty fun with my group and I love "prophunt" games, but I would only ever play this game with a group of friends. I don't recommend unless you have a group to play with.

Why you should or should not play MECCHA CHAMELEON What is MECCHA CHAMELEON? In this game your goal is to play an intense game of hide and seek except the hiders have the ability to change what they look like in order to blend in with their surroundings. Quick Pros ✔ Unique mechanics ✔ Easy to learn controls ✔ Lots of different playstyles ✔ High replayability ✔ Active multiplayer community ✔ Fun cooperative gameplay ✔ Competitive modes are engaging ✔ Friendly player community Quick Cons ✘ Limited gameplay variety ✘ Matchmaking issues Initial Thoughts: -"OMG Look at me I am doing it! I AM PAINTING ON MYSELF" -"You are never going to find me- oh the host left." -"Okay- seriously this is the best hiding spot I have ever had you are never- oh you found me." Beginner Player Friendly? I would argue this game is very beginner player friendly, it is a good price and has all the controls clearly labeled on the screen so you dont have to stress about forgetting something. This is also something your whole family can play. Game Play: The gameplay is simple- you want to hide, but also somehow in plain sight. You can either be a hunter or the hunted, if you are the hunter you have to find the others who have hidden around the map. And if you are the hunted or as the game calls you the 'Chameleons' then you have to paint your character to hide in the enviornments provided. There is not much more to it then that. Graphics: The graphics are simple and make sense for the style of game this is, if they were more complex then what it is now it may make it harder to hide in these enviornments. However, I am sure with the steam workshop being connected to this game there will be some pretty awesome maps coming out very soon and I have already seen a minecraft one popping up all over the place. I believe that this game could run on lower end systems. Community: Everyone on this game seems super fun even with the added voice chat. I have not run into anyone that isnt just there for a good time. So far this community all seems very genuine. How long can you play? So far I have only played for a limited amount of time and that is only because I was on a time crunch and had to work the next day. I think however this game would be super fun to play when you are having a drink and hanging out with friends. The playtime potential is very high and I think I have yet to put a few more hours into this game. My Opinion: In my opinion, this game is super fun and has a simple concept. I would argue it can be more fun with friends, however do not let that detour you from giving this game a try. Overall Review ★ 7/10 - Paint to survive, even if you are hiding in plain site... or as a horses scrotum. ✔ Active Online Multiplayer (meaning people will interact with you in game) ✔ Family Friendly (meaning friendly to all ages) ✘ Solo Playability (meaning you can play by yourself if preferred) ✘ Cross Compatibility (meaning you can play with friends through other consoles.) ✘ In-App Purchases (meaning you can buy things after buying the game i.e DLC's) ✘ Controller Compatible (meaning you can play this game with a controller) Enjoyed this review? This game is an easy way to kill a few hours with some friends. The movement is a little goofy, and there are some spots hiders can fit that can make it very difficult, if not impossible, to find or hit, but the anti clip system helps with this quite a bit. With mods supported, I imagine custom maps will also add more variation and replayability to the game. My only complaint is that the server search system feels a bit goofy, has an excess of slurs and profanity, and Steam invites don't seem to work. With all that in mind, I'd say it's worth the $5 price tag.

This game has a good concept, but it executes it quite poorly. First of all, the optimization in this game is atrocious; the movement is janky, the fps constantly stutters and spikes, my ping even spiked to 12000ms at some point, and my internet definitely isn't notorious for acting up like that in games. The game itself can be fun, but there's a couple of reasons it's not in my opinion. 1. Poor replayability: The game offers 4 official maps, with more maps existing in the workshop. Most of these maps are detailed, cluttered, and large, making it difficult for the Hider to find a good place to hide, and for the Hunter to look through every spot and crevice. Most of the time you will not win a game as the Hider unless you are in a hiding spot that is covered by something else, in a corner, or somewhere inaccessible. 2. Poorly designed UI: The UI is.. definitely not visually appealing to say the least. Working the painting system seems more like a chore than an enjoyable task, and the painting options are extremely limited. The eyedropper might be accurate pixel-wise, but when it comes to application it seems completely different thanks to shadows and lighting always being off. Having the server owner launch a workshop map completely breaks the game if you don't want to download it, thanks to there being absolutely zero X button. 3. Janky, clunky, and cumbersome. The movement feels terrible, you move one place and it pushes you off onto another. You can't turn around properly while hiding because the game loves to overwrite your rotation, you have to phase yourself into a wall to be able to climb up and there are so many bugs in the game that it feels demotivating having to deal with them every other round. I have problems with motion sickness in games, and because of this clunky movement (and the lack of an FOV slider) I'm not able to play the game for more than a couple minutes at a time without feeling sick. Sick concept, bad execution.
This was incredibly fun for the first time playing with my friends, 3 hours of absolute mind boggling torment. If you suck at seeking you might just have it better hiding!

Concept and Overall Experience (9/10) Overall, this is a really, really fun game, particularly if you have a large group of people (around 7-10 players). I would definitely recommend it! The maps are large and visually noisy, so there are many places to hide. You can paint yourself any color with sliders to make each color more metallic or rough, so as long as you can draw decently, you can hide well enough to get by. There is basic customization (game length and timed taunts) and it seems to be balanced pretty well gameplay-wise. The concept is perfect for a friend-slop style game. Most importantly: it leads to countless funny moments if you have a good crew! That said, I would definitely only recommend playing with people you know. On the downside, the large and noisy maps make it less fun if you have fewer players because it can take forever to find a talented hider, or even a decent one. With only a few people dispersed around the map you could spend several minutes each round without seeing a soul. You should have enough people to start with two hunters and still have several hiders for the best experience. Maps (7/10) The maps as I said are fairly large, best for 7-10 players. I think they are designed really well and have a lot of fun elements! That said, I wish they were smaller. The maps have a good combination of noisy and simple elements where a variety of colors and textures are found, but I do wish the maps would focus on being more compact and filled with interesting and noisy elements and less on a sprawling prophunt-like layout. The best moments in this game is when someone gets away with hiding in an absurdly obvious spot because they painted themselves well, which happens infrequently when the maps are this large and sprawling. For funny and effective spots, I appreciate the signs, paintings, and little odd-ball objects most. Optimization (2/10) As wonderful as the game is, it runs terribly. The in-game voice chat was horribly glitchy, so I just used Discord, and the game itself uses more computer resources than I imagine it needs. My fan was running extremely loud for what seems like a relatively simple game. The graphics are simple, and the servers do not host an insane number of people (I had 10 players). The painting would be the most computationally expensive part, but even that should just be modifying a UV map. I also experienced a lot of lag/movement issues when players overused their guns, which required me and a few others to restart (the glitches didn't stop even after the guns were not in use). Optimization is where it is lacking most, which to be fair is not surprising for a small game. Smaller Features and Polish (5/10) There are some features that could be polished. The UI is definitely messy and is not laid out in an easy-to-use way, a bit chaotic. The controls are also a bit unintuitive and do not follow a clear scheme or pattern. The paint controls for "roughness" and "metallic" could be adjusted; roughness doesn't seem to do much on its own. The eye dropper tool also could be improved to have clearer options for grabbing the material color and rendered pixel color separately. Right now, they are both a bit inaccurate and stored in two different tools. The poses that players can be in are also a bit limited. There are just a few, and I think the game would benefit from several, maybe 7 or 8. Player movement is also a bit glitchy and inconsistent. Lastly, I think the game needs to be stricter with the collision mechanics so that you cannot hide in impossibly small gaps so that the gameplay can lean into the 'blending in' aspects more than just plain hiding. Right now, most players can hide inside a gap instead of painting themselves very little. End Note: Awesome game! I do not know if this game will have further development, but I hope it will! With some polish and optimization this honestly could be one of the best games I've ever played. It's mainly just the performance, UI, and a bunch of little features that could use improvement. Stuff like that is always a hassle to code and playtest, but it would be worthwhile if the developer(s) are planning to update the game. Overall, I love it and strongly recommend it if you have a good crowd! :D

I've played a lot of prop hunt style games in my time. Most of them are, frankly, about as imaginative as a HR departments PowerPoint presentation... But every so often, something comes along that makes you sit up and think: yes. Yes, this is it. Meccha Chameleon is, on the surface, a Prop Hunt game. You know the sort.. hide as an object, hope nobody notices you're actually a slightly suspicious-looking lamp. The problem with most of these games is that they're repetitive and undeniably dull. You've become a bin! Wonderful. Now what? Meccha Chameleon, however, asks a rather more interesting question. What if, instead of becoming an object, you simply painted yourself to match your surroundings? It's genius and it genuinely makes me wonder why no one came up with this sooner. This game genuinely surprised me, because while the human eye may indeed be a marvel of engineering. It is fooled with remarkable ease by even the most ham-fisted attempt at camouflage. A few daubs of the right colour in roughly the right place and suddenly you're invisible. And that's important, because I think a lot of people will be turned away at the idea of this amazing game, simply because they're acutely aware that they're not the next Picasso. But it doesn't matter! You would be surprised by just how easy it is to hide in plain site. Like a reform voter (or maga supporter) at a convention for victims of brain damage. The result is a game that is original, genuinely rewarding, and, when played with friends, an absolute riot. It's a top-tier game. The only way they could improve on this, is by adding some more brushes for the paint system, and more maps! 9/10 - An example of excellence in originality and fun!

The main problem I find with this game is the amount of time you spend as a hider sitting around and hoping that you aren't caught. Finding a hiding spot and colouring your character is incredibly fun, but afterwards I mostly found myself waiting around, or maybe even pulling out my phone out of boredom. I think it would be interesting if there was a mode where each player set a hiding spot and coloured their character only at the beginning of the round, and then every player spawns back in as a hunter and is tasked with exploring the map while trying to find everyone else's spots. This way, every player gets to flex their hiding and hunting skills each round, and little to no waiting around occurs. But overall I think this is a great concept for a game and I really enjoy how it gets you thinking about different textures and patterns and how you can blend in with them.

Right now, it feels much more like an Early Access title than a finished release. The biggest issue is that the core mechanic, painting yourself to blend into the environment, doesn't work reliably. The game gives you a pipette tool to select colors, but around 90% of the time it picks the wrong color, even when you're clearly clicking on the shade you want. On top of that, if any part of your body is slightly clipping into an object, the game reveals your position to hunters with a red flash. As a result, most of the times I got caught weren't because the hunters outplayed me, but because I spent 30 to 60 seconds trying to position my character so it wasn't colliding with the environment or fighting with the color picker to get the correct shade. By the time I finally managed either of those things, the hunter had already found me. What's even stranger is that many of the players who survived the longest weren't actually using the painting system effectively at all. They would often just paint themselves a dark color and hide in shadows, blending into the lighting rather than matching the environment itself. When the game's main mechanic is camouflage, that mechanic needs to be smooth and reliable. Unfortunately, in its current state, it isn't.

I kind of enjoyed this game, but since Steam doesn't have a neutral rating, I'll give it a thumbs up. I really missed playing Prop Hunt. At one point, I even downloaded some random mobile Prop Hunt clone just to scratch that itch. MECCHA CHAMELEON puts a fresh spin on the genre by moving away from disguising yourself as props. Instead, it's all about how well you blend into your surroundings. You can squeeze yourself into tight hiding spots, though experienced hunters will probably know where to look, or you can paint yourself to match the environment and hide in plain sight where they'd least expect it. One thing I wish the game had is a "copy wall" feature for the paintbrush. A true chameleon should be able to instantly mimic the pattern of whatever surface it's attached to, instead of manually copying colors and hoping it's convincing enough to fool the hunters. I also don't know if it's just me, but playing as the hunter can get a bit boring. I think the role would benefit from more variety—different weapons instead of just shotguns, and maybe limited grenades to deal with those frustrating hiding spots that hunters can't easily reach. My biggest complaint is the lack of dedicated servers. You either host your own lobby or join someone else's, and if the host has a bad connection, everyone suffers from lag. I can absolutely see this game being a blast with friends. In random 24-player lobbies, though, you'd better awaken your ultra instinct and come up with the perfect hiding spot—otherwise, you'll just get showered with random bullets.

Well. It's fun. That's it. Now let's talk about problems (and potential problems) Problems: 1- Voice chat, idk it's the game or players, but some players sound buggy, and you can hear that they speak something, but can't hear what. 2- There are russians in that game (not so much, but enough to be annoying). And they are, well, russians. Hate speech, dirt talk about parents, loud dirt talk music in the mic. 3- Eye dropper copy color much darker than it is on the texture. takes time to manually adjust to match the background. 4- Only a few maps, so very soon all good hide spots will be well known. Hope there will be more. (Yes, paint camo, it's the point of the game, and it's great. But space is still limited, and hiding in plain sight is not for everyone) 5- Little of the net freezes. Timers stop after a few seconds, it catches up with the actual time. But for that freeze, you can't trust game timers. Potential problems: 1- All maps are actually one map. When (I hope) there will be more maps, it will be an optimisation problem. Maps separated into different worlds are for a reason. 2- Server browsing needs to be enhanced. You click to connect - loading - you are back to the main menu without any explanation - happened more than successful connections. Tag system - usually ignored. 3- No player and bug report systems. It will be necessary if this game survives after the first wave will lose interest. Well. Despite problems, the game is cool. Hope devs will make it better. Good luck and have fun.

Had a blast with my friends tonight. Was really fun and we all had a great time playing it. The only three things I wouldn't mind/suggest would be the addition of a light tool, a scale tool, and the ability to fiddle with character poses. Could be a ball/orb around your character so you can control/match the lighting of the spot you hid in angle and hue. Could make the objects/light engine apply to the character as well when they are near a shadow/light source if you wanted to avoid another tool. Was hard to match the color of a box on the wall to hide quickly on the shorter timer rounds we did. Which I know is part of the fun but we all mentioned getting the right colors was difficult. A yellow we picked up from the wall could be a very bright tan on the body that didn't match the wall. Wouldn't mind a scale tool to tuck in spots a little easier. Nothing to dramatic like shrinking down to a pixel but maybe a preset small medium and large option. Would love if we could move the bodies around to conform with things to hide even better like moving them to hug a ball, pillar, or a corner for example. Maybe an easier way to rotate our characters while we are in paint mode or something. All in all though we had fun. Was a great quick little venture we spent a few hours on. Looking forward to playing more with them. We all did notice the uh.... colorful lobby names in the public though.

It took a few games for me and my friends to get the hang of it, but it is overall a pretty good time. I was worried at first that it would be too much like prop hunt and not have enough to differentiate itself, but the painting mechanic can actually be really intuitive if used well. For our first few games we tried cramming ourselves in corners and coloring ourselves all dark browns and grays, which was effective enough. But its actually a lot more effective to hide in plain sight and rely on bright colors and details that are easier to miss at a glance. Plenty of times within just a few hours we were facepalming at how we missed people hidden right in plain sight. That said, the game has it's flaws. The UI is awful. The way everything is always on screen just looks very unappealing and it needs some real work. The physics can also be janky and I dont like that you can just climb up walls and stick yourself to the ceiling. There need to be some limits to how people can move around and hide themselves. The core gameplay is really good and if its flaws can be addressed then its a really promising game.

Meccha Chameleon is basically Prop Hunt, except instead of turning into objects, you paint yourself into the environment. 8/10 It sounds simple, but it turns into chaos immediately. I played it with a friend first before bringing in my usual group, and it quickly became one of those sessions where nobody is really playing properly because everyone is laughing too much. You’re technically hiding, but mostly you’re panicking while trying to repaint yourself into something that vaguely resembles the map. One round stuck with me. I was hunting my friend in a bathroom. Checked every corner, peered into stalls, went over everything twice because there just weren’t enough hiding spots for him to be missing. At one point I genuinely thought he might’ve become a plunger. Then I found him. He had painted himself into the wall tiles. The colors were slightly off and the pattern didn’t fully match, but I still walked past him multiple times before it clicked. It just didn’t register at all. That’s the game in a nutshell. The painting system is the core mechanic, and it’s also where things get messy. Matching colors under pressure isn’t very precise, so most players end up improvising and hoping “close enough” works. Sometimes it does. Sometimes it absolutely doesn’t. Either way, everyone ends up looking like they’ve been partially blended into the scenery. It’s chaotic, a bit janky, and not especially polished but that’s part of the fun. Every round turns into people making increasingly questionable disguises and hoping nobody notices. Meccha Chameleon works because it consistently creates moments where obvious things somehow get missed until it’s too late. It’s simple, funny, and great with friends. Just don’t trust your friends near bathrooms. If I was to suggest anything the painting system could use some tightening. More accurate color matching and slightly better blending tools would make intentional disguises feel less random and more “skill-based”. That said, the slight jank actually adds to the comedy, so I wouldn’t want it fully smoothed out just enough polish to reduce frustration without killing the chaos that makes it funny.

I was really excited for this game when it was announced but unfortunately I ended up disappointed. I understand that "Meccha Chameleon" was developed by only two people and I genuinely appreciate the creativity behind it. The idea is awesome and feels fresh compared to most multiplayer games, concept of hiding through camouflage and blending into the environment is very unique and has a lot of potential!! However, I'm surprised the game launched as a full release rather than in "Early Access" because it currently feels unfinished in several important areas. The UI is difficult to navigate and finding or connecting to servers can be really frustrating. There's also no way to invite friends through Steam which makes playing together more complicated than it should be. Painting system is one of the game most important mechanics but it still needs work. Brush behavior can be inconsistent and the color picker tool often doesn't work properly. I would also like to see improvements to the painting system overall, as well as reconsidering the unlimited ammunition mechanic. Controls and gameplay mechanics can be confusing for new players and there are still technical issues such as crashes and exploits. Players can hide inside or between certain objects in ways that make them impossible to shoot, which takes away from the "camouflage" aspect of the game. Because of this matches often feel more like a traditional hide and seek game rather than a game focused on "creative blending" and disguise. Object collision and map geometry definitely need a rework. Another major issue is the lack of moderation tools. There appears to be no chat filter, reporting system or option to disable chat. As a result it's common to encounter players spamming slurs, making sexist comments, harassment, bullying and even death or rape threats. If implementing chat moderation is difficult, at the very least there should be reporting features or an option to mute/disable chat entirely. I also think the game would benefit from some visual adjustments. Simpler color palettes and less busy lighting on maps could make camouflage gameplay more readable and enjoyable. More maps would also help keep the experience fresh. At its current price, the game can still be fun and the core concept is genuinely clever. However the technical issues, lack of polish, moderation problems and unfinished mechanics make it difficult for me to recommend right now. I hope the developers continue improving it because there is a cool game hidden underneath all of these issues.

Very fun game! I'm the artist of my friend groups and the two friends I played with were shocked at my hiding places. Different graphics game modes are very interesting. Gameplay quality: fun, easy to learn, consistent. Replayability: near infinite. Play to your heart's content. Bugs: Surprisingly not many. Very new game, some are expected. One of my friends got behind a shelf in a corner where literally no one could shoot him. But other than that not problematic. Cons: There are no different types of brushes, but it is acceptable because dithering is possible and effective and brush size is very adjustable. The drawing mesh is slightly pixel-y when drawing details. It can be very hard to shoot someone if they decide to run around. There are NO settings to look at or change keybinds. No apparent reporting system yet. Pro: skill based prop hunter. my friends are also becoming artists too. I'm very proud of them. If I haven't convinced you yet, buy the game, and get your friends to buy it.

I do still have gripes with the lighting and color picker though. the color picker should 100% get an average within a small radius instead of the exact pixel (that also seems to change if there's a reflection.) Also a lot of things reflect light a lot for some odd reason despite those things not really being an object that should reflect light like that. You can clearly see in the trailer when they're painting themselves on the bricks that the bricks are shiny for some reason. You can also see how painting yourself with a strong reflection is really obvious due to your character being curved at the end of the trailer. Although the spot they chose there shouldn't be somewhere you hide anyways but it's the best example I can give since you can't add pictures to reviews or comments. the game doesn't absolutely need simplistic color palettes in maps just as long as actually painting yourself is made a little better. I'm not a huge fan of how your paint doesn't go around the body unless you can actively see that part of your body which can make painting something simple much slower than it has to be. also a fill tool would be extremely nice for quickly setting a base for your character. Also changing the size of the brush is weird and has something to do with the position your cursor is when you're trying to change it and could probably just be binded to something that increases/decreases it when pressed. I do think this game has a ton of potential still and with a few changes i could easily recommend it

I don't review often but I'm seeing way too many negative reviews for what i think is easily the best hide and seek video game there is. I just would only highly recommend the game if you're going to play privately with friends currently. In Gmod hide and seek, every spot is basically determined by the map you play on and not your own creativity, and winning feels more like you were lucky because the seeker didn't check your spot. Prop Hunt improves that by allowing you to disguise yourself as a prop, but if you play the intended way and just blend in with other props, it feels like the same problem as regular hide and seek where you just hope you aren't shot at instead of the other props you're blending in with, which is why i mostly see people hiding as small bottles and just hiding in corners. But in this game, your spots feel truly your own, the ability to essentially hide anywhere as long as you can paint well enough makes everywhere a potential hiding spot, making spam shooting as a seeker essentially useless. if you're creative enough, you don't even have to simply blend in with a wall or anything, you can paint yourself into details that never even existed and get away with it, like the time i painted myself as a light bulb above a door, a bush in a pot, and even made my character do the sitting pose and hid as the letter L on a wall. It allows so much player expression in how they play and the strategies for where they hide, and makes the maps feel infinitely replayable. Naturally though there's some things that need to be fixed, and the best way i can put it is that the game basically necessitates monopoly-style house rules to keep it fun and fair. i think some maps (the sewer map especially,) has some nooks that are basically impossible to spot people hiding in because of the shadows (the wave breakers being the biggest culprit,) and i feel like a lot of the default rules are not balanced enough around the hiders like the 60 second hide time being too short. these are only really issues if you play on public servers i assume since with friends you can just ban shadow hiding, but it's still a massive issue, any game with the ability to play public servers should be designed around that, but it's why i stated i only highly recommend this game if you're playing with friends. The game has a workshop for custom maps and I am VERY excited to see what will come of it, but in the ones i've played the shadow issue is even worse than the official maps, which again makes playing without house rules impossible. This right here is the pinnacle of virtual hide and seek, and with friends it's already a blast, but it has some blatant issues that needs ironing out. but for a silly little indie game i got for 5 bucks? absolutely worth it. UPDATE 6/13/2026: the 1.2.0 update fixes the issue with loading custom maps AND adds an incentive to not just hide in shadowy corners by giving you a rank depending on how long and how close a seeker looks right at you without seeing you. considering it hasn't even been a week and they made these changes and more, AND added a whole new map, i'm even more impressed with this game and recommend it even more

I wanted to play this game so badly, I had seen the videos before release and it looked so fun. I finally got the game when it came out but I was never able to play with my friends because the UI so awfully horrible. It won't let you invite your friends to a lobby you're in, the "Invite to Game" feature doesn't work, its broken. There are so many servers, even with applying filters it won't show the same servers as your friends looking, and there is no search button to find them, so it's incredibly hard to find and get the same server up as your friends to join. After it would take ~5 minutes each time to find a server that appeared on both of our lists (again with 4 filters applied to narrow down the search) we would both join a lobby, then you see that the game is already in progress so you have to wait for it to finish, then after the game finally finished and we would get to play it would kick us both out of the lobby, with no error message or explanation whatsoever. This happened about 4 times. 4 times after we FINALLY were able to get into a lobby together and waiting the entire time for the current game to finish, it would just quick us out, no explanation, nothing. We spent 30+ minutes through this entire process just to try and play 1 single game. We never even got to play together, I am not going to play a game solo as it seems its best and designed with friends. Being that I never got into a game, I cannot speak to the gameplay and how fun it may be, but the UI and trying to find a game with your friends is so horrible that its incredibly hard just to play. For these reasons I cannot currently recommend the game, maybe in the future when they fix these issues I will try to come back, but in its current state it is literally unplayable. We could never get into 1 game. Good idea in practice for a game, but it definitely needs cleaning up before being worth getting.

I originally couldn’t recommend this game due to the severe motion sickness it gave me, despite setting motion blur to 0 %. However, I downloaded a crosshair HUD overlay that helped immensely, as the static overlay helps anchor my view. Now I can actually enjoy the game, and I find it quite entertaining and creative. As others have mentioned, the game needs quite a lot of refining. The UI, controls and mechanics are somewhat confusing, and the servers are very unstable. Developers seem active though, and they already updated some of the UI elements. Due to the limited amount of maps, players have already learned different ways to clip through and behind objects, making it impossible for hunters to catch them. This makes the experience slightly less enjoyable, as the game feels more rigged that way. However, I just switch servers if I notice a lot of players doing this. All in all, it’s an original take on the hide and seek genre, and with the HUD overlay, I can actually enjoy it. I hope the developers fix the issues in the game to make it even better. Immediate wishlist: 3rd person view as hunter (helps with motion sickness), softer brushes, ability to create more textures/finishes than just metallic, ability to stick to diagonal surfaces, more maps, a rework of the maps that fill up any hollow areas unreachable to hunters.

Review may change later, but for now I gotta be really honest. The game IS FUN, but it is super half baked and I really think it should've stayed in development for at least another few months. - The UI is awful, its tacky in an ugly way, you can't change the UI to be bigger or smaller, which is a shame cuz some of it is really hard to read. - The server search is horrible and it took us 30 minutes to figure out how to join a private lobby. - And worst of all, the game is extremely unoptimized. I know you can't expect optimization from an unreal engine game, but it shouldn't crash multiple times throughout a session. I had all my settings to the lowest possible, yet every time I created a lobby - I'd crash. Every time I would accidently turn on the 3D color picker - I'd crash. On complete bloody random - crash. It got infuriating to a point because, with what the game is, it should NOT be this horrible unoptimized to the point it's crashing this often. The graphics are not anything special so my guess is that it really is just not something the devs really cared about. Lower spec PCs just can't run this game. - The coloring UI needs some work, the colors almost never look like what they are on the body so it becomes incredibly difficult to manually change up the colors. - Some maps are just far too big for small groups, it would be nice if there were more smaller maps like the indoor farm or whatever its called. Right now the mansion and the sewers are way too hard for small groups of people. The game has a lot of potential and its REALLY fun when you get it to work, but in its current state I would say save yourself the headache and just wait until its a little bit more cooked. Hoping the devs take note of these issues and fix them, cuz if they do, theyll have a real gem of a game.

One issue I've noticed is with the voice chat system. When a match is already in progress and a new player joins the lobby, they enter spectator mode but can still move around and use voice chat. This allows them to reveal the hiding spots of other players to the hunters, which can ruin the game and create an unfair advantage. I think it would be better if players who join while a round is in progress are unable to speak or freely spectate until the current round ends. Other than that, I've enjoyed my time with the game and I'm looking forward to future updates. Recommended.

The concept of this game is great. But the execution in this case is not. Aside from the overall jank of the game, the execution of the concept in this game is flawed. The maps doesn't allow much room for you to blend into the environment with paint and camo. Instead of surfaces with texture and gribbles (example: wall crown molding, pipes, etc), you got some rooms filled with random small stuff and plain walls that looks flat. This make it not ideal for you to blend in with camo, as the shadow thickness of your doll man would give it away at the first 2 second of looking at them. And instead, this only encourage player to find cheap hiding spot in between stuff, just like regular prop hunt (only worse and less interesting since the gimmick of this game is the camo paint). The camo painting system is alright, but lack some QoL. And UI is just bad. Overall, this game need some time to cook in the oven. Even then, not sure if they can salvage this, as the core execution is flawed (like the map design).

Ratings: * Gameplay Concept: 8.8/10 * Hider Experience: 8.5/10 * Seeker Experience: 6.9/10 * Creativity Potential: 9.2/10 * UI & Accessibility: 5.8/10 Meccha Chameleon is a lot of fun when people play it the way it's intended. The painting mechanic is unique, and playing as the tiny hiders is hilarious. I especially like the different poses and the optional seeker vision filters that make camouflage a real strategy. My biggest issue is that many players ignore the painting system entirely and hide in tiny dark cracks or nearly impossible-to-see spots. This turns matches into "check every crevice" instead of rewarding creative camouflage. Because painted players are often spotted instantly, I understand why people do it, but it hurts the core gameplay. The UI and server browser need work. Joining the same lobby with friends is a hassle because everyone has to manually scroll through the server list looking for the lobby name someone calls out. The menus feel more complicated than they need to be, and there is no easy way to join friends. I've also experienced occasional freezes lasting around 10–15 seconds in game. Public lobbies can be rough as well. Usernames, server names, and chat are often filled with profanity and outright racist content with little apparent moderation. The game also needs better lobby flow. I've joined fresh matches only to spend 10 minutes spectating while seekers hunt down the last few players. Restricting joins to round transitions or adding smarter matchmaking would help. That said, the game has a ton of potential if the developers continue pushing the camouflage aspect and open up more creative hiding options. I'd love to see abilities that allow flatter poses and improvements to prop hitboxes to encourage clever hiding rather than cheesy hiding. The foundation is excellent, it just needs refinement to fully deliver on its unique premise.

I recommend this game, but with some caveats. I love the game, its fun, exciting in the best ways when its playing right. I love getting a really good paint in and watching players scramble around me. That being said they really need to fix the clipping issues/notifications. The penalty for clipping is too sensitive making it really risky to try to use ball form or seated form in anything but very specific ways. I dont have an issue with the flashy warning and penatly, but it's too sensitive, especially when the player model is 80-90% visible. It also doesn't always warn you. Quite often you can be invested in a spot and get all the way to painting and the game will only then warn you that you are clipped too far into something. I have had the game flash me for sitting in a toilet in ball form. I even jumped to make sure I hadnt forced myself down into the model. Whenever this happens it leaves almost no time to recover and you lose your whole round.

This review is Negative because of the developers, publishers, and more importantly the people who purchased the game. It has nearly nothing to do with the release state of the Early Access title, while some points of note will be mentioned. Do not waste your time buying it, as the game will not receive further updates (besides a possible few bug fixes by those willing to keep it running) according to the developers. I encountered myself, little to no issues with the game. It ran quite poorly, but the first 2 patches (for me personally) fixed most of the performance issues. And while I have seen issues through other people, some of which are very game breaking, people need to remember that it's an EARLY ACCESS $40 title. They could have charged more, but didn't. They could have promised us the MOON like No Man's Sky, but didn't. While I agree some things were a bit misleading, this game did not deserve the treatment that it got from players. People were calling it a scam BEFORE they ever played it or bought it. It was doomed for failure because of the whiney crybaby internet. While simple at it's core, do some quests from your tablet, make some money, buy some gear, and go kill some people; the game had more in it than similar titles like Escape from Tarkov when they first released into their Early Access, and had arguably more complexity to it than fully priced releases such as No Man's Sky. Yet people loved these games so much (be they developers in the case of No Man's Sky, or players in the case of Tarkov), that they're now both perceived very highly and have become amazing games in their own rights. This review is mostly a reflection of the Developer and Publisher response to the bad reviews. They immediately decided to cut their losses and say that they can no longer operate. PLENTY of companies operate millions of dollars in debt for YEARS. I am disappointed in the team for making this decision. The game was fun. It had amazing potential. It was much more accessible than Tarkov, which could have sky rocketed it's success had they stuck with it. Most reviewers played for less than an hour, which is barely enough time to get through the introduction and maybe do a SINGLE excursion to the city. They didn't even try it or give it a shot. They played the intro and just said, nah it's a scam, without even playing it. And everyone who reviewed it like that should be ashamed of themselves. Comments are enabled just so I can watch you all lose your minds that anybody thought this game wasn't that bad. I hope they read this and see that not everybody hated it. They should have given it a little bit of time, even if it hurt them until it helped them.

There are many different ways a game fails upon release, for example, No Mans Sky had a very rough start, from lies to an unpleasant lack of game play, but they put in so much work to deliver what was originally promised, and even though it took a while to get there, they still managed to make a fun game with a lot of different aspects in regard to gameplay/creativity. But then you have a game like The Day Before, many lies, awful game, and overall, a failure to the community, all leading to show that this game is a complete SCAM. This game has been in "development" for years, just to come out as a game showing no effort or care. There are many MANY things that went wrong with this game, I won't go over everything as I have not done a load of research about all of the issues, but from what I have seen there are loads of bugs, possibly AI voice-overs, faking gameplay/alleged plagiarism in the trailers, delays, volunteers instead of employees to avoid paying for staff, and a bunch of other stuff (all over the Reddit and articles). If you look on the r/TheDayBefore sub-Reddit, there is a post showing all the asset packs from the Unreal Engine marketplace. Now there is no issue with using these assets, but the issue lies when FNTASTIC say that they are not asset flipping and lying to the community making out they are making everything themselves. At this point, it's obvious that FNTASTIC wanted to put something together, put the game out, and just run with the money, after 4 days of the release, they shut down the game and are trying to remove themselves from the internet and hide away. The Day Before had a lot of potential especially as one of the top-wishlisted games, but the FNTASTIC team was the cause of the downfall of this game. The game can no longer be purchased, and I wanted to keep the game rather than refund it as a reminder of the failure that happened here, also because it's just a funny L.

I did not have a chance to play the game. The servers were full and the game was loaded with servers issues. Fortunately, I was made aware of other negative reviews early enough to request a refund. After seeing the official statement of closure from the developer, it is clear there is an industry standard for developers to rely on "Early Access funding" for games, which is incredibly disappointing. I understand creating a game is not only costly, but also takes an incredible amount of time and effort; however, I believe if this game remained in development to deliver what was promised at Full Release, this would have been a success. I had high hopes for this game. I hope the failure of this development is a lesson for other studios its critical to release what you promise. Calling a half-finished game "early access" and still charging full price, expecting the community to be happy has become a risky practice.

•The Day Before is a game that never should have gotten this much hate.• +When the game was put into development, fntastic, the company that was behind the production of the game, used a team of volunteers to make the game. The volunteers were never paid for their work, they were only provided benefits when working on the game. The head of fntastic was run by two brothers named Eduard and Aisen Gotovtsev from Russia who were in charge of the production of the game and what kind of trailers were okay to make and release to represent the game. When it came down to making these decisions, the bothers were very indecisive when I came to make these decisions. It was so bad that the brothers copied other trailer layouts like Red Dead 2 and Call of Duty and they made the team of volunteers remake the game four times until it was okay by their standards. It got to a point when If the team spoke up to the brothers about certain problems with the game then that volunteer was either dropped from the company or shunned by them. When the game was released, it was set in an early-access stage because the team of Fntastic wanted to ensure this game met its expectations from its trailers. Being an open-world Player V.S. Player V.S. Environment-type game where you go anywhere you want whether by walking out into the game or quickly traveling to your plot of land, with no extractions spots and dealing with herds of zombies. To ensure that this game lived up to these expectations, the Fntastic team needed help from its community to remove any type of bugs or glitches that the players might encounter while building the game up from its early access stage. As this was happening, famous content creators like THERADBRAD, ASMONGOLD, and PENGUINZ0. They started playing the game only once when it had just been released in the early access stage, slandering the game as a scam through content they made and posted on social media and never played it again, stating that the game was not an MMO and that Fanactic lied about it being an open world game. + ×However, it clearly states in the Early Access Game section on the Steams page that “Besides bug fixes and improvements to balance and core gameplay, we plan to introduce additional content to the game, such as new items, weapons, equipment, cars, and houses.” and “The game is in Early Access because this is our first huge game at Fntastic, and there may be unforeseen circumstances. Early Access also allows us to refine and improve the game in collaboration with the Community.”× +This just proves that the game was never a scam, and famous content creators used this game to build their popularity.+ ×It also states that "This Early Access game is not complete and may or may not change further. If you are not excited to play this game in its current state, then you should wait to see if the game progresses further in development."× +As the game was getting bombarded by people who just gave nondescriptive reviews. The Fntastics team made two hot fixes to stop the majority of glitches from happening. However, the brothers had other planes. On the fourth day of the game's release, the company shut down due to the brothers who stole around five million dollars from their partners and left, Leaving the game hanging to dry.+ •From this whole thing I can say that this is more than just another glass ball scam like Fushigi. Okay. Despite all the drama with the devs this game is not that bad. The volunteers made good assets and the fictional brands in the game are pretty fun to look at. The main menu music is spectacular. There are good minor details in the game (such as zombies jumping out of a car) and the overall player movement, when not lagging or skipping, is pretty functional. Taking it for what it is, an early access extraction shooter, it's pretty good. I'm not defending Fntastic in any capacity. I'm just hoping, HOPING, that another game company buys this game, fixes all its problems, maybe changes the genre a little bit and re-releases it. There is greatness lying under this rotten meat. And I'm begging to see it. I refunded the game obviously but I can't help but feel a little bit of regret. I'll recommend the game but not for this price. Not for any price for that matter. But it was a fun 3 days. EDIT: Feel free to hate this game, I am not defending Fntastic and their ways. I do not care about the devs. I want a good survival game. There are good moments and ideas in this game made with love by a few volunteers they had. If it was made by a proper dev team such as Konami or the old Naughty Dog it would have been a good game. But it is made by Fntastic, so it's not.

I think the game had a lot of potential, but offered nothing different than countless other (better) games that are exactly like it. It's unfortunate, and i actually feel bad, but regardless of the normal early access bugs the game was nowhere near ready to be released. Zombies take multiple head shots to kill with the starter rifle. You start with a full kit (Clothes, food, drinks, weapons, backpack, etc) which isn't typical to survival games. There wasn't much loot to be found anywhere. I wandered the entirety of the lower portion of the map (the city) and encountered 3 zombies, and zero other players. The U.I. was weird. In order to extract in one zone i had to leave everything behind, but in another zone it was safe and underground and i could extract with everything, didn't make sense unless i was being chased by players. The graphics were good, but the voice to character sync was slow and inaccurate. (Mouth movement when they spoke) Following the map was very difficult as there were zero cues as to where to go to complete specific mission objectives.

They Released "The Day Before" and Closed "The Day After"

I'm only leaving a positive review because even its still yet in a vaporware state, it still did had shown a nice shell to present. THE PERSPECTIVE: So, The Day Before in a nutshell, from my POV after trying it, looks like the following mix, -A The Last of Us UI & menu style with a Character Creation to fit it -An Uncharted Loading screen whit a spinning coin. -An Escape From Tarkov copy and paste of the loot & extraction system -The Division Mapping but with looting areas heavily PVP focused.. -A Home system mimicking any house builder game whiteout any real purpose or usage to the player. SUMMARY: All this could had been a nice 3rd person/first person extraction shooter, but in reality, it is simply an empty re-purposed zombie shooter sprinkled with an extraction system and some pre-made assets all around without any core mechanics really finished and some clear game unbalances. In its current state, if the company is really shutting down already and is checking for bankruptcy, id highly suggest another studio with the funds to go ahead re-buy it, and do so, the leftovers of this game might be salvageable if reworked for a year or two since its still basically an asset flip game, and i do think so because i do know one or two game with the Synty's asset Store that did delivered. (Motor Town is a good example of a well done asset flip game). SCORE: 3/10

To say I was disappointed with The Day Before would be a gross understatement. I’m not here to bash the game and tell you how terrible it is or try to be humorous about it’s glitches and poor quality. All I can do is provide my experience as someone who’s been playing video games for over 20 years and has played thousands of them to this day. I’ve seen one other situation where something like this happened - Aliens: Colonial Marines. And even Gearbox got off pretty scot-free, due to the huge popularity of the Borderlands franchise at the time. Fntastic lied. Plain and simple. They showcased dishonest and unrealistic demonstrations of The Day Before in trailers. They lied about stealing ideas from other companies. They lied about using third-party assets. They lied about their workplace culture. Fntastic was plagued by higher-ups who sold us, the gaming community, a giant lie for two and a half years. The Day Before was supposed to the next “breakthrough survival MMO”, in the genre. Those were Fntastic employees’ words in the scarce screen time they actually talked about the game. What did we get? A disgraceful, broken mess of an empty, extraction shooter that looked like it was thrown together in 3 months, at best. This game wasn’t even ready to be released in alpha. It needed YEARS of more work to even RESEMBLE the many terrific gameplay trailers they showed off. I, like many other people, fell for their marketing. And I’m ashamed to say it. It’s severely harmed my ability to trust smaller, indie studios that take on huge ambitious projects. Everything surrounding The Day Before boils down to one thing and one thing only: Nobody works for free. And if they do, they’re probably hiding something from you. There are no “volunteers” in this business. This is a business, first and foremost. And iconic studios/companies, like Naughty Dog, Larian Studios, Insomniac, Sucker Punch, Ubisoft, Nintendo, Rockstar, and Sony Santa Monica, among others, know this business. Whether you love them or hate them. Fntastic shut down as of today. They proved that they bit off more than they can chew. And now, what little money they actually have that paid their salary employees, are now going to their debtors. The Day Before will rank in gaming history as probably the strangest game of all time. Most wishlisted on Steam of all time. A myriad of legal troubles and copyright issues. Only to shut down 4 days later after their product of “5 years of blood, sweat, and tears” released. A year plagued with layoffs. Closing out on a sour note. Do not buy The Day Before. This game and studio are dead. As they should be, unfortunately. Thanks for coming to my TED talk.

While I had hoped for a positive experience, unfortunately, this game did not meet my expectations. The cityscape, advertised as vibrant, turned out to be a desolate area with just a few zombies and no other players despite the server being almost complete. Additionally, there was a noticeable delay in bullet hits, making it feel like the attacks were ineffective, taking away from the immersive experience I hoped for. Unfortunately, shortly after the game's release, the developers announced the shutdown of the entire project, with 90% of the community already having refunded their purchases due to widespread dissatisfaction. Clearly, the developers didn't deliver on their promises, and the game was simply an unfinished and buggy product released to make some quick cash. While I wouldn't recommend purchasing this game, plenty of other promising alternatives in the gaming market are worth exploring. I believe that constructive feedback and criticism can help developers improve their games and deliver better experiences in the future.

FNTASTIC Studio closed up shop 4 days after the release of this game that they kept putting off for the better part of 3 years. Nothing in their announcement videos, or game play videos looks or feels like the game they released. Moreover, this seems to have been a trend with this company releasing a gaming project, taking in initial funds and then abandoning the project. This game was the most anticipated game of the year but this company severely fell short on delivering a solid product. Starting with the server based online multiplayer. That in itself was a very poor judgment call. No Melee mechanics, Costs of items are ridiculously high, No story or plot to follow or appreciate, attachments for guns and ammo was lazy and confusing. Devs seemed to really favor attachments for the Deprov. Barely any attachments for pistols or snipers, what attachments were available, you had to go to a workbench to attach something that typically would be able to snap on or screw on in the field. Realism was non existent. This game truly let me down. I was hoping this would be the next "Last of Us". Honestly, instead of scrapping the project, they should GIVE it to Naughty Dog and have it be the game it was supposed to be. Extremely disappointing. Yes I am aware bad games happen, but those of us who are fans of LoU and have been following this game since inception with the idea that it would be as advertised, that is why its taken to heart. This game has/had a lot of potential but lacked the drive and willpower of the devs to really see it through.

I was firmly in the "this game isnt great, isnt worth $40, and needs major server help... but it's got potential" camp... until the studio closed down the doors on day 3. I've refunded it. Still has potential -- needs VOIP, RPG elements, and work on hit registration -- but that's to be expected with an early access game. But now that the studio is shut down, I'll wait for another dev team to buy the IP (and offer a massive 50+% off sale) before jumping back in.

The Studio FNTASTIC Games who made this title released the game on the 7th of December to then put our a public Tweet that the studio will be closing down only 4 days after it's release. https://twitter.com/FntasticHQ/status/1734265789237338453/photo/1 This seems very odd and more like a cash grab than anything else before they closed the doors. Here is what was written by FNTASTIC in the Tweet. "Today, we announce the closure of Fntastic Studio, Unfortunately, The Day Before has failed financially, and we lack the funds to continue. All income received is being used to pay off debts to our partners. We invested all our efforts, resources, and man-hours into the development of The Day Before, which was our first huge game. We really wanted to release new patches to reveal the full potential of the game, but unfortunately, we don't have the funding to continue the work. It's important to note that we didn't take any money from the public during the development of The Day Before; there were no pre-orders or crowdfunding campaigns. We worked tirelessly for five years, pouring our blood, sweat, and tears into the game. At the moment, the future of The Day Before and Propnight is unknown, but the servers will remain operational. We apologize if we didn't meet your expectations. We did everything within our power, but unfortunately, we miscalculated our capabilities. Creating games is an incredibly challenging endeavor. We're grateful to everyone who supported us during these difficult years. It's been a fantastic journey over the past eight years: 2015: Opening of the studio 2017: Release of The Wild Eight 2018: Release of Dead Dozen 2018: Release of Radiant One 2021: Release of Propnight 2023: Release of The Day Before" As you have read they new in advance that the studio was going to shutdown and shouldn't have cash grabbed a release that hasn't even been our a week. After much anticipation, my experience with "The Day Before" has been nothing short of a monumental disappointment. The game, which promised an immersive and groundbreaking experience, falls embarrassingly short of its lofty promises. Firstly, the disparity between the initial marketing and the actual gameplay is jarring. The trailers showcased a vibrant, detailed world with innovative mechanics, but what I received was a hollow shell of those assurances. The gameplay mechanics, a critical component of any game, are equally underwhelming. The promised fluidity and realism are absent, replaced by clunky and unresponsive controls that detract from the overall experience. The AI, touted to be intelligent and challenging, is instead predictable and uninspired. Moreover, the narrative and storytelling, essential elements that were supposed to set "The Day Before" apart, are superficial and poorly executed. The plot is riddled with clichés and lacks the depth and engagement that was so passionately promised. To add insult to injury, the game is plagued with technical issues. From frequent crashes to game-breaking bugs, it's evident that "The Day Before" was rushed to release without proper testing and refinement. In conclusion, "The Day Before" is a prime example of how hype and marketing can overshadow the reality of a product. It's a game that promised the moon but delivered a mere pebble. I feel misled and regret my purchase.

The Day Before: A Masterclass in Pre-Apocalyptic Disappointment If I could give this game a lower rating, I would, but unfortunately, one star is the pitiful limit. "The Day Before" is a shining example of how to turn anticipation into pure regret. It's like they took all the excitement for the apocalypse and crushed it under the weight of their own incompetence. Let's start with the character design. I've seen more realistic faces in a preschool finger-painting class. It's as if the developers tried to create characters based on descriptions of humans given by aliens who have never actually seen one. If awkward facial expressions and stiff animations were the goal, then congratulations, they nailed it. And don't even get me started on the gameplay mechanics. Trying to control your character feels like herding cats, blindfolded, with oven mitts on. It's like the developers thought, "What if we make the controls as clunky and unresponsive as possible?" Well, mission accomplished. I've never felt more disconnected from a virtual world, and I once played a game where the main character was a sentient toaster. The crafting system is a whole different disaster. It's so convoluted that I needed a PhD in engineering just to figure out how to duct-tape a couple of sticks together. The crafting menu is a labyrinth of confusion, and half the time, the items you need mysteriously disappear, probably into the void where the game's potential went. The open-world concept is a joke. The world is about as lively as a graveyard, and not the cool, zombie-filled kind you'd expect in an apocalypse game. It's just you, the occasional tumbleweed, and the haunting echoes of your own disappointment. But perhaps my favorite part of this debacle is the way the game handles survival. Spoiler alert: you won't survive. Not because of the zombies or the lack of resources, but because your will to live is obliterated by the sheer pointlessness of it all. It's like the game itself is trying to convince you that life isn't worth living in a world where "The Day Before" exists. And let's not forget the glitches. Oh, the glitches! It's like the developers decided to embrace the chaos. NPCs moonwalk through walls, cars defy the laws of physics, and zombies perform interpretive dance routines instead of attacking you. It's so entertainingly broken that I almost forgot I paid actual money for this disaster. In conclusion, "The Day Before" is a perfect example of how to take a promising concept and execute it so poorly that it becomes a cautionary tale for future game developers. If you enjoy frustration, confusion, and an overwhelming sense of regret, then by all means, give this game a shot. For the rest of us, save your money and invest in something more rewarding, like a pet rock or a collection of lint from your dryer. Anything would be more satisfying than subjecting yourself to the catastrophe that is "The Day Before." - ChatGPT 2023

The Studio is closing days after Early Access.. nuff said... Today, we announce the closure of Fntastic studio. Unfortunately, The Day Before has failed financially, and we lack the funds to continue. All income received is being used to pay off debts to our partners. We invested all our efforts, resources, and man-hours into the development of The Day Before, which was our first huge game. We really wanted to release new patches to reveal the full potential of the game, but unfortunately, we don't have the funding to continue the work. It's important to note that we didn't take any money from the public during the development of The Day Before; there were no pre-orders or crowdfunding campaigns. We worked tirelessly for five years, pouring our blood, sweat, and tears into the game. At the moment, the future of The Day Before and Propnight is unknown, but the servers will remain operational. We apologize if we didn't meet your expectations. We did everything within our power, but unfortunately, we miscalculated our capabilities. Creating games is an incredibly challenging endeavor. We're grateful to everyone who supported us during these difficult years. It's been a fantastic journey over the past eight years: 2015: Opening of the studio 2017: Release of The Wild Eight 2018: Release of Dead Dozen 2018: Release of Radiant One 2021: Release of Propnight 2023: Release of The Day Before FNTASTIC

I came in not expecting much, and did find not much. Graphically, it's honestly decent. I'm not familiar with the Unreal asset store to say if this is an asset flip, but even if it was, I did not see any two assets that didn't go together, so at least props for that. The city is quite big and even if the view distance isn't huge even on max settings, it felt immersive enough. I did not have any performance issues, the game was running at 120+ fps on a 3080 with max settings. The story starts like the generic zombie game story, no idea if it evolves into something but it was neither bad or good, there's voice acting with some facial animations, the npcs have some dialog options to learn more about the world or story and can give you small tips, nothing much but it did help giving some sense of coherence to the world. But well, that was the best I had to say about this game... It's all downhill from there. First the gameplay, while it technically functions, it's really lacking in the enjoyment part. The movement is as basic as it gets, you can run but you'll get stuck on any small ledge, you can jump but you need stamina for that, of course. Not sure if it consumed stamina, but when I was out, no jumping for me. Then there's the lack of melee, a lot of people are complaining, and rightfully so, if you ever run out of ammo or find yourself with no equipment whatsoever for some reason (foreshadowing), then you're as good as dead : you cannot outrun zombies nor fight back, you might get lucky and get them stuck on some decor piece, but you'll eventually run out of stamina and get hit at least once, which inflicts bleeding, slowly killing you. And then there's the world itself, it is pretty sure, but gosh is it empty. All throughout the 40 minutes I managed to spend actually playing, I saw a grand total of... *drum roll* 11 zombies ! Woohoo ! And I went inside buildings to loot, firing my gun left and right in the streets, occasionally finding a couple zombies. During this entire time, I was hearing gun shots constantly from other players, I do not know how large the range is, but I never managed to find any player. I ran, and ran, and ran, stopped to regen stamina on some slightly elevated decor which zombies don't know how to climb, and then resumed running. I did loot some stuff on my way, but let's talk about that : a select few random assets you'll come by will have an interaction prompt to open them. I could not find any common pattern between them, sometimes an asset could be looted, sometimes not, sometimes you had a pile of 20+ crates and not a single one could be opened, sometimes there's an icecream cart with ammo inside. It's impossible to tell beforehand if it'll be lootable, and you'll only know once you're up against the thing you're trying to loot. All that for a handful of random items that didn't seem to have any relation to what you were looting, so no strategy as to where to go or what to prioritize, making it a fairly bad looting experience. The gunplay wasn't too bad but it also wasn't particularly impressive, the only issue I had with it was from the lag. Oh yeah, I did not mention it yet, but joining a server is hardcore. I could not manage to join any in Europe, nor North America, but did eventually manage to join Asian ones. I'm saying eventually because that's what the 30 other minutes of play time are, selecting a character, waiting 10s for the server list to refresh, scrolling alllll the way to the bottom to find a server that wasn't full, selecting join and waiting 10s, being booted back to the main menu with an error message, and repeat. Again. And again. Each time taking me about 15 minutes to manage to join a server. So of course playing on a server in a different region isn't great, the lag ranged from actually not bad to about 1s delay on everything. Wouldn't be as much of an issue with a joining queue or more servers in my region, but alas the team seemed unprepared for accomodating many players, ironic for an MMO. Also "MMO", as I mentionned, I haven't seen a single player outside of the hub area, a few friends I was playing with did see some apparently, but after some research it appears that you can at max play with 32 other players in the same instance. To put it in perspective PUBG isn't considered an MMO and you can fight against 100 players in a single match. While on the subject ; the open world portion is as much open world as an extraction shooter goes. There are a handufl of random buildings you can enter, the map seems fairly large though I feel like you could explore most of it in under 30 hours of playtime. Did I say I was playing with friends ? Sorry, I meant to say all 4 of us bought the game but played each on our own because no matter how much we tried, joining the same server was impossible. The others played for a couple hours more than I did, and not once did we manage to have two people in the same server. Quite the epic experience for a multiplayer game. Let's not forgot to mention that the game is quite unstable, on average you should expect a crash every hour or so, or even faster sometimes. And everytime it does it's back to joining hell of course. Oh but that's not the worst part, when you disconnect, your avatar stays there for 60s before you're actually logged out. Do you care to guess what can happen during those 60 seconds in game where you loose all your stuff upon dying ? That's right, if there was a zombie or another palyer nearby, you'll die. And lose everything in your inventory. Well, if it's a zombie, it should all be on the ground for you to loot, that is if you manage to join the same server again haha, good luck with that, I couldn't manage that. So yeah, I ran, saw nothing, ran, looted a bunch of random stuff, ran, finally found a building I could go in that looked kinda interesting, crashed 5 minutes after, joined back and was in the middle of the city with nothing, no ammo, no armor, no weapons, no bandages, no food, just the 500 coins you get after dying. So I ran towards the nearest exit point, came across one zombie, ran, eventually got caught up and took a hit, started bleeding, ran, regen stamina, ran, found another zombie, ran, and died of bleeding not even halfway to the exit point. There are vehicles in the game supposedly, but the cheapest you can buy costs 1,100,000 credits. To ground that number, the most expensive weapons cost in the thousands, up to like 20 thousands or so. And those will already take you multiple trips to buy. So I hope you have freed up your schedule over the next couples months if you want one. No Idea if you can steal them from other players, or if there are any lying out in the open, but I could not interact with any of the vehicles I came across. I tried looking into all the shops to see where the game could go, but as far as I could tell, there really isn't much beyond a bunch of weapons, a few attachments, some armor and backpacks. I don't know where they plan on taking the game, but at least for now there doesn't seem to be much to progress towards, again don't know if the story is any better than the typical MMO fetch quest excuses of a story. In conclusion, whether it's for the lack of gameplay, lack of content, server issues, or expectations towards the promised experience, I'd urge you to stay away from this, at least for now. The game didn't feel like a scam to me, there clearly was effort put into it, maybe not 5 years though but it still felt like an alpha of an actual game. At least if the game was $10 or so, maybe I would've kept it and tried it again every once in a while when an update seems to bring something interesting. But $40 ?!! For this lackluster shell ?! Yeah no... Come back in another 5 years and maybe it'll be interesting then.
